title: The Paradox of Mutual Reasonableness
subtitle: How Iran and the US claim fairness while rejecting each other's ceasefire terms
body_md:
Both sides of the Iran-US ceasefire talks claim reasonableness while dismissing the other's proposal as categorically unacceptable. Trump labeled Iran's counter-proposal "totally unacceptable," according to [CBC World](https://www.cbc.ca/news/world/iran-us-ceasefire-drone-attacks-9.7194290?cmp=rss), while Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei characterized Tehran's terms as "reasonable and generous" in statements to [Iran International](https://www.iranintl.com/en/202605112092), describing Iran as "a responsible power in the region" that "stand[s] against bullies." The deadlock is so complete that each side frames its own position as the rational baseline and the other's as unreasonable—a rhetorical symmetry that suggests convergence may be impossible.

The broader story that most outlets converge on is the Strait of Hormuz closure and its ripple effects. The blockade has shifted global oil markets and forced a reckoning with maritime vulnerability. Most coverage treats the Strait as the central lever: a functioning passageway means negotiated settlement; a closed one means escalation or prolonged crisis. But beyond this shared framing, the story splinters dramatically along geographic lines. Western security analysts and policymakers focus on the military asymmetry and operational risk. Asian economies center the spillover damage. And Iran's domestic media—both state and opposition—frame the talks through the lens of national concessions.

The security-focused frame dominates Western outlets. Germany's military experts warned that "Iran must only succeed once to trigger a catastrophe," according to [Spiegel International](https://www.spiegel.de/international/world/u-s-military-expert-on-oil-tanker-convoys-in-the-strait-of-hormuz-iran-must-only-succeed-once-to-trigger-a-catastrophe-a-1b4f4521-01dd-4711-8a5e-8da225d053b5#ref=rss), framing the blockade as an asymmetric vulnerability that works against Iran's long-term interests. India's shipping reports tracked an Iranian-linked LPG tanker transiting Hormuz under a false flag, as [Republic World](https://www.republicworld.com/world-news/iran-linked-lpg-tanker-transits-hormuz-identifying-itself-as-indian-owned) reported, emphasizing the tactical cat-and-mouse of sanctions evasion. These framings treat the Strait as a chokepoint that Iran can only weaponize temporarily before international coalition pressure restores flow—a narrative that assumes Western military capacity will ultimately override Iranian closure.

Asia-Pacific outlets, by contrast, treated the blockade as an economic emergency requiring regional coordination. ASEAN leaders called for the restoration of "safe, unimpeded, and continuous transit passage" according to [The Diplomat](https://thediplomat.com/2026/05/asean-leaders-call-for-middle-east-calm-reopening-of-strait-of-hormuz/), and moved to ratify a joint petroleum security framework, shifting the framing from military deterrence to collective energy survival. The same outlet noted that "Asia sources around 60 percent of its crude oil imports from the Gulf," making the region acutely dependent on Hormuz reopening. This framing is less about military capability and more about economic interdependence and institutional response.

The paradox itself—mutual rejection despite simultaneous claims of reasonableness—reveals how framing deadlock undermines negotiation language. Iran framed its proposal as including "ending the war in the region, lifting what [it] described as the US blockade, releasing frozen Iranian assets, ensuring safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz," while Washington prioritized "a deal to end the war, reopen the Strait of Hormuz and roll back Iran's nuclear program." Both claim to offer a ceasefire; neither accepts the other's definition of what "ending the war" means. Trump's rejection as "totally unacceptable" suggests the gap is not a negotiable difference but a categorical incompatibility.

What's notably silent: Russia and China's deeper positioning on proposal content. While Western and Iranian media debated the terms, Russian and Chinese outlets focused on the economic interests at stake—China's access to Iranian oil and maritime routes—rather than engaging with the diplomatic substance. Brazil covered regional military responses and Saudi Arabia's role more than ceasefire proposal analysis. These silences suggest that Moscow and Beijing may be hedging their framing until a settlement looks more likely, treating proposal rhetoric as tactical rather than substantive.

The stakes are immediate and geographically uneven. Oil prices have spiked, benefiting producers like Saudi Arabia—Aramco's first-quarter profit jumped 26 percent, as [Egypt Independent](https://www.egyptindependent.com/saudis-arabias-oil-giant-gets-boost-from-higher-crude-prices/) reported—while squeezing consumers in Asia and the developing world. The longer the Strait remains closed, the more Asia's energy security depends on either Iranian cooperation or international coalition action to restore flow. Neither looks imminent if both sides can simultaneously claim reasonableness while categorically rejecting the other.
